evals.prompt: I have a Node.js monorepo with packages in packages/api and packages/web. I want CI that only runs tests for the package that actually changed, not both every time. Also needs to work on PRs and pushes to main.
evals.expected_output: A workflow YAML that uses path filtering (either native paths: filter or dorny/paths-filter) to conditionally run separate jobs for api and web. Should include caching, proper permissions, and handle both push and PR triggers.

assertions.description: Uses path filtering mechanism to detect which packages changed
Has separate CI jobs for api and web packages
Includes dependency caching
Declares explicit permissions block
Handles both push and pull_request triggers
Provides a complete, valid workflow YAML

evals.prompt:
My GitHub Actions workflow keeps failing with 'Resource not accessible by integration' when trying to post a comment on a PR. Here's my workflow:

name: PR Check
on: pull_request
jobs:
  comment:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/github-script@v7
        with:
          script: |
            await github.rest.issues.createComment({
              owner: context.repo.owner,
              repo: context.repo.repo,
              issue_number: context.issue.number,
              body: 'CI passed!'
            });
evals.expected_output: Diagnose the missing permissions block — the workflow needs 'permissions: pull-requests: write'. Explain why this happens (default token permissions may be read-only) and show the corrected workflow.

assertions.description: Correctly identifies missing permissions as the root cause
Shows pull-requests: write or issues: write permission in the fix
Provides a complete corrected workflow YAML
Explains why the default GITHUB_TOKEN may lack write permissions
Pins the action to a SHA or recommends doing so

evals.prompt: I want to set up a deployment pipeline for my Python FastAPI app. It should: run tests on every PR, deploy to staging automatically when merging to main, and deploy to production only after manual approval. We use AWS ECS. I don't want to store long-lived AWS credentials as secrets.
evals.expected_output: A multi-job workflow with: test job, staging deploy job using OIDC for AWS auth (id-token: write permission, aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials with role-to-assume), production deploy job with environment protection rules requiring manual approval. Should include concurrency groups to prevent racing deploys.

assertions.description: Uses OIDC authentication for AWS (no stored access keys)
Declares id-token: write permission
Uses GitHub Environments for staging and production
Production deploy requires manual approval
Includes concurrency groups to prevent racing deploys
References ECS deployment mechanism
Runs tests on pull requests

evals.prompt: I need to run my integration tests against a PostgreSQL database in GitHub Actions. My app is a Python Django project. The tests need a real Postgres instance, not SQLite. How do I set this up?
evals.expected_output: A workflow that uses service containers to run PostgreSQL alongside the test job. Should include health checks on the Postgres container, proper environment variable setup for Django's DATABASE_URL, Python caching, and correct port mapping. Should explain the localhost vs service-name hostname difference.

assertions.description: Uses a services: block with a PostgreSQL image
Includes health check options for the Postgres service
Maps port 5432 correctly
Sets DATABASE_URL or equivalent env vars for Django to connect
Sets up Python with caching
Provides a complete workflow YAML

evals.prompt: I'm reviewing our GitHub Actions workflows and I'm worried about security. We have several workflows that use `pull_request_target` to comment on PRs from forks. One of them checks out the PR code and runs the test suite. Is this safe? What should I look for?
evals.expected_output: Explain the critical security risk: pull_request_target + checkout of PR code = untrusted code running with base repo's write permissions. Should describe the attack vector (malicious PR can exfiltrate secrets), explain when pull_request_target is safe (metadata-only operations) vs dangerous (executing PR code), and recommend the workflow_run pattern as a secure alternative. Should mention script injection risks with PR-controlled contexts.

assertions.description: Clearly identifies that checking out PR code under pull_request_target is dangerous
Explains the attack: untrusted code runs with base repo permissions and can steal secrets
Explains when pull_request_target IS safe (metadata only, no code checkout)
Recommends workflow_run pattern as a secure alternative for running tests then commenting
Mentions script injection risks with untrusted PR contexts (title, body, branch name)
